---
title: "Function Basics"
description: "Define functions, pass arguments, return results, and understand scope."
execute:
  echo: true
---

## Why this matters

Define functions, pass arguments, return results, and understand scope.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one mental model.

::: {.questions}
### Questions

By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Function Basics** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.learning-objectives}
### Learning objectives

You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 4 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- Section 1: **Defining and Calling Functions** — Create named reusable blocks with def and call them when needed.
- Section 2: **Parameters and Arguments** — Pass information into functions using parameters and arguments.
- Section 3: **Return Values** — Send a result from a function back to the caller.
- Section 4: **Scope** — Understand where variable names can be used.

<!-- mermaid-visual-model -->
## Visual model

::: {.visual-model}
A function call moves information into a named block of code and may send one
result back to the caller.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  caller["caller code"] -- arguments --> params["parameters<br/>inside function"]
  params --> body["function body"]
  body -- return value --> caller
  body -. local names stay inside .-> scope["local scope"]
```

Read every function by asking: what arguments go in, what local names are used,
and what value comes back?
:::

## Section 1: Defining and Calling Functions

Create named reusable blocks with def and call them when needed.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Defining a function is writing a recipe card; calling it is using the recipe to cook.
:::

### Mental model

A function definition teaches Python a new action; a function call asks Python to
perform that action.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
def greet():
    print("Hello!")

greet()
```

### Walkthrough

1. `def greet():` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print("Hello!")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `greet()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
Defining a function does not run it. Add parentheses after the function name to call it.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "functions-defining-calling-functions",
  "title": "Defining and Calling Functions checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Defining and Calling Functions?",
      "options": [
        "A function definition teaches Python a new action; a function call asks Python to perform that action.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: A function definition teaches Python a new action; a function call asks Python to perform that action."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Defining a function is writing a recipe card; calling it is using the recipe to cook."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 2: Parameters and Arguments

Pass information into functions using parameters and arguments.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Parameters are blanks on a form; arguments are the values you write into those blanks.
:::

### Mental model

Parameters are names in a function definition; arguments are actual values supplied in a
call.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
def greet(name):
    print(f"Hello, {name}!")

greet("Ada")
```

### Walkthrough

1. `def greet(name):` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `print(f"Hello, {name}!")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `greet("Ada")` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
A missing required argument raises TypeError. Count the blanks in the function
definition.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "functions-parameters-arguments",
  "title": "Parameters and Arguments checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Parameters and Arguments?",
      "options": [
        "Parameters are names in a function definition; arguments are actual values supplied in a call.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Parameters are names in a function definition; arguments are actual values supplied in a call."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Parameters are blanks on a form; arguments are the values you write into those blanks."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 3: Return Values

Send a result from a function back to the caller.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

A return value is like a receipt from a machine: after the machine finishes, it gives
you something to use later.
:::

### Mental model

return hands one value back to the code that called the function.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
def double(number):
    return number * 2

result = double(5)
print(result)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `def double(number):` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `return number * 2` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `result = double(5)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(result)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
print() displays a value, but return gives a value back to the program. They are not the
same job.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "functions-return-values",
  "title": "Return Values checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Return Values?",
      "options": [
        "return hands one value back to the code that called the function.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: return hands one value back to the code that called the function."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: A return value is like a receipt from a machine: after the machine finishes, it gives you something to use later."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Section 4: Scope

Understand where variable names can be used.

::: {.analogy}
### Analogy for this section

Scope is like rooms in a building: a note on your desk is easy to see in your room, but
not in every room.
:::

### Mental model

Scope is the region of code where a name is visible.

### Tiny example

Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
message = "outside"

def show():
    message = "inside"
    print(message)

show()
print(message)
```

### Walkthrough

1. `message = "outside"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
2. `def show():` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
3. `message = "inside"` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
4. `print(message)` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
5. `show()` — read this line slowly and ask what value or action it creates.
6. Continue the same process for the remaining lines, one line at a time.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.challenge}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy the tiny example into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.debugging-corner}
A local variable inside a function is not automatically available outside it. Return
values you need later.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Section checkpoint

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "functions-scope",
  "title": "Scope checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Scope?",
      "options": [
        "Scope is the region of code where a name is visible.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Scope is the region of code where a name is visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Scope is like rooms in a building: a note on your desk is easy to see in your room, but not in every room."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after the tiny example works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
tiny example, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.key-points}
- **Defining and Calling Functions**: A function definition teaches Python a new action; a function call asks Python to
perform that action.
- **Parameters and Arguments**: Parameters are names in a function definition; arguments are actual values supplied in a
call.
- **Return Values**: return hands one value back to the code that called the function.
- **Scope**: Scope is the region of code where a name is visible.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- Defining functions: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/controlflow.html#defining-functions
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
